The economic and social impact of child labor worldwide

Author

Aloïs Fallard

Introduction: The Persistent Challenge of Child Labor

Child labor remains a major global concern. Despite progress made in recent decades, millions of children are still forced into work that robs them of their childhood, their potential, and their dignity, and that is detrimental to their physical and mental development.

This report sheds light on the current state of child labor (ages 5-17) around the world, exploring its links to macroeconomic factors such as Gross Domestic Product (GDP) and life expectancy, using UNICEF data.

1. A Global Overview of Child Labor

To understand the scale of the problem, it is essential to map the distribution of child labor across the globe. The map below illustrates the percentage of children involved in economic activities and domestic work, based on the latest available data by country.

2. The most affected countries

While the problem is global, it is unevenly distributed. In-depth analysis reveals that some countries have alarming rates. The following bar chart highlights the 10 countries with the highest rates of child labor. These regions often require emergency interventions and increased international support.

3. The weight of the economy: GDP vs. Child labor

One of the fundamental questions is whether a nation’s poverty determines its rate of child labor. Cross-referencing the child labor indicator with GDP per capita reveals an interesting correlation. Generally, countries with lower GDP per capita tend to have a higher proportion of child labor, as shown in the regression line below.

4. Temporal evolution of life expectancy in vulnerable countries

Child labor and economic hardship often have repercussions on the long-term health of populations. If we look at life expectancy over time globally, we can see a steady overall improvement. This timeline illustrates the evolution of average global life expectancy over the past few decades.

Conclusion

The data is clear, ending child labour requires more than just laws; it demands a global commitment to economic stability and universal education. By bridging the financial gap for vulnerable families and focusing on high-risk demographic zones, we can return this 160 million children to the classrooms.
